s2.q: Preterm labor is defined as regular uterine contractions associated with cervical change occurring between:
s2.explain: Preterm labor is defined as regular uterine contractions with cervical change between 20 and 37 weeks' gestation. The 20-week lower bound distinguishes it from late miscarriage.

s2.q: What distinguishes true preterm labor from Braxton-Hicks contractions?
options: Effacement and dilatation of the cervix
s2.explain: Effacement and dilatation of the cervix distinguishes true labor from Braxton-Hicks contractions. Regular contractions alone are not sufficient for diagnosis.

s2.q: A premature infant is defined as an infant born:
s2.explain: A premature infant is an infant born before 37 completed weeks of gestation.

s2.q: Which of the following best defines this condition?
options: A pathological deviation from normal anatomy or function
An incidental finding with no clinical significance
s2.explain: A definition describes the essential nature of a condition as a deviation from normal.

s2.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s2.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s3.q: What is the most common cause of preterm labor?
s3.explain: The most common cause of preterm labor is idiopathic — i.e., when no specific risk factor is identified, idiopathic remains the leading cause.

s3.q: Which of the following is NOT a maternal risk factor for preterm labor?
s3.explain: Fetal congenital anomalies are a fetal cause/risk factor, not a maternal one. Maternal risk factors include previous preterm labor, infection, uterine anomalies, smoking, obesity, overdistension, maternal age extremes, and bleeding.

s3.q: Uterine overdistension as a maternal risk factor for PTL can result from:
s3.explain: Multiple gestations (twins, triplets) and polyhydramnios mechanically stretch the myometrium, leading to uterine overdistension — a recognized maternal risk factor for preterm labor.

s3.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s3.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.
Multiple maternal factors contribute to obstetric risk.

s4.q: Which of the following are fetal causes/risk factors for preterm labor?
s4.explain: Fetal causes of preterm labor include fetal anomalies (congenital structural malformations) and intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR).

s4.q: A compromised fetus may trigger earlier delivery in preterm labor through which fetal risk factor?
s4.explain: IUGR (intrauterine growth restriction) — a compromised fetus may trigger earlier delivery as a fetal cause of preterm labor.

s4.q: Congenital structural malformations associated with preterm labor include all EXCEPT:
s4.explain: Adrenal hypoplasia is associated with postterm pregnancy (diminished fetal cortisol response), not preterm labor. Neural tube defects and cardiac anomalies are fetal causes of PTL.

s4.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s4.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.
Multiple maternal factors contribute to obstetric risk.

s5.q: According to the textbook, the contraction-frequency threshold that defines active preterm labor is:
options: 4 contractions in 20 minutes or 8 in 60 minutes
s5.explain: The '4 in 20 / 8 in 60' rule is the textbook definition of active preterm labor — this frequency triggers tocolysis and corticosteroids.

s5.q: Which of the following is a symptom/sign of preterm labor?
s5.explain: Symptoms and signs of PTL include uterine contractions (4 in 20 min or 8 in 60 min), lower back pain, passage of blood-stained vaginal discharge (show), sensation of pelvic pressure, and bulging membranes / rupture of membranes.

s5.q: The sensation of pelvic pressure in preterm labor is described as:
options: Sharp stabbing pain in the upper abdomen
Feeling that the fetus is pushing down
Constant dull ache in the right flank
s5.explain: Sensation of pelvic pressure is described as a feeling that the fetus is pushing down — a classic symptom of preterm labor.

s5.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s5.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s5.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s5.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s6.q: The normal shape of the cervix on transvaginal ultrasound progresses through which sequence as preterm labor approaches?
s6.explain: The cervical shape progression is T → Y → V → U. A U-shaped cervix on TVUS indicates imminent delivery.

s6.q: What is the diagnostic window for the fetal fibronectin test in predicting preterm labor?
s6.explain: The fetal fibronectin test is useful only between 24 and 34 weeks. Before 24 weeks, fibronectin may normally be present; after 34 weeks, its presence is no longer predictive.

s6.q: On transvaginal ultrasound, a cervical dilatation greater than what measurement suggests preterm labor?
s6.explain: Transvaginal ultrasound showing dilatation of cervix > 2 cm is suggestive of preterm labor.

s6.q: The gold standard diagnostic test is:
s6.explain: The best diagnostic method varies by condition — some rely on imaging, others on lab tests or clinical findings.
Choice of investigation depends on the suspected condition.

s7.q: Which of the following is a prophylactic measure for preterm labor in women with cervical incompetence?
s7.explain: Cerclage surgery (e.g., McDonald or Shirodkar suture) is placed prophylactically in women with a history of cervical insufficiency, typically at 12–14 weeks, and removed at 36–37 weeks.

s7.q: Prophylactic management of preterm labor includes all of the following EXCEPT:
s7.explain: Prophylactic measures include stopping smoking, cerclage surgery (if cervical incompetence), and progesterone. IV tocolysis is part of curative/acute management, not prophylaxis.

s7.q: At what gestational age is a prophylactic cerclage typically placed?
s7.explain: Cerclage is placed prophylactically at 12–14 weeks and removed at 36–37 weeks.

s7.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s7.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s7.q: Which factor most influences the choice of treatment?
options: All of the above — treatment is individualized
s7.explain: Obstetric management is tailored to gestational age, maternal status, and available resources.

s8.q: The three drug therapy pillars in curative management of established preterm labor are:
s8.explain: In established PTL, the three drug-therapy pillars are corticosteroids (for fetal lung maturity), tocolytics (to delay delivery 48 hours), and antibiotics (for GBS prophylaxis).

s8.q: Why are tocolytics given alongside corticosteroids in preterm labor management?
options: To buy 48 hours for corticosteroids to act on fetal lung maturity
s8.explain: Corticosteroids need ~48 hours to complete their effect on fetal lung surfactant production. Tocolytics are given to delay delivery for those 48 hours.

s8.q: Bed rest as part of curative management of PTL works by:
options: Reducing gravitational and mechanical stimulus on the cervix
s8.explain: Bed rest reduces the gravitational and mechanical stimulus on the cervix, which may delay delivery.

s8.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s8.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s8.q: Which factor most influences the choice of treatment?
options: All of the above — treatment is individualized
s8.explain: Obstetric management is tailored to gestational age, maternal status, and available resources.

s9.q: The recommended dose of betamethasone for fetal lung maturity is:
s9.explain: Betamethasone is given as 2 doses of 12 mg IM, 24 hours apart.

s9.q: The recommended dose of dexamethasone for fetal lung maturity is:
s9.explain: Dexamethasone is given as 4 doses of 6 mg IM, 12 hours apart.

s9.q: Corticosteroids in preterm labor decrease the risk of which neonatal complication?
s9.explain: Corticosteroids decrease the chance of respiratory distress syndrome (RDS) and other prematurity complications including IVH, NEC, and neonatal mortality.

s9.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s9.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s9.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s9.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s12.q: Which antibiotic is used for GBS prophylaxis in established preterm labor?
s12.explain: Benzyl penicillin or ampicillin should be given for established PTL as prophylaxis against early onset neonatal sepsis due to group B streptococcus. In penicillin allergy, clindamycin can be used.

s12.q: In a preterm labor patient with breech presentation at 32 weeks, the recommended mode of delivery is:
s12.explain: For breech presentation before 34 weeks, cesarean section is done. Vaginal delivery is allowed for breech only if more than 34 weeks.

s12.q: For preterm labor with cephalic presentation, the preferred mode of delivery is:
s12.explain: With cephalic presentation, vaginal delivery is the preferred mode even if delivery is at < 34 weeks.

s12.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s12.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s12.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s12.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.
